data.title: Anthropic wins court order blocking U.S. ban on its AI technology
data.description:
Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) won a court order blocking a Trump administration ban on government use of its artificial intelligence technology, after arguing the move could cost the company billions in lost revenue.

U.S. District Judge Rita F. Lin issued a preliminary injunction on Thursday, halting the administration’s plan to cut ties with Anthropic while the case proceeds in San Francisco federal court. The order was stayed for seven days to allow the government time to appeal.

The dispute stems from a Defense Department determination earlier this month that Anthropic posed a threat to the U.S. supply chain, escalating tensions over how the military can use AI tools. Anthropic (ANTHRO) had sought assurances its technology would not be used for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons deployment, while the government cited national security concerns and rejected any restrictions.

In her ruling, Lin questioned the basis for the ban, saying it did not appear to serve national security interests. She suggested the measures were aimed at penalizing the company, calling them “classic illegal First Amendment retaliation.”

Anthropic (ANTHRO) said it welcomed the decision, adding it remains focused on “working productively with the government to ensure all Americans benefit from safe, reliable AI.”

The company argues it was excluded from government contracts for its stance on AI safeguards, while the administration has signaled it will continue to pursue efforts to remove Anthropic from federal agencies. Government lawyers said trust is critical in military partnerships and accused Anthropic of undermining that trust during negotiations.

The case also extends to a parallel challenge filed in a Washington, D.C., appellate court, where Anthropic claims the Defense Department exceeded its authority with actions that were “arbitrary, capricious and an abuse of discretion.”

Anthropic (ANTHRO) signed a $200M contract with the United States Department of Defense in July, becoming the first lab to deploy its models in mission workflows on classified networks. Rivals OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and xAI (X.AI) also received DoD awards worth up to $200M last year.

In other news, Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) is weighing an initial public offering as soon as October, as it competes with rival OpenAI (OPENAI) to go public.

data.title: Anthropic weighs IPO as soon as October amid race with OpenAI, report says
data.description:
Anthropic PBC (ANTHRO) is weighing an initial public offering as soon as October, Bloomberg reported, citing sources, as it competes with rival OpenAI (OPENAI) to go public.

The company has held early talks with Wall Street banks about leading roles on a potential listing, with Goldman Sachs (GS), JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Morgan Stanley (MS) among those expected to be considered.

A listing could raise more than $60B, according to The Information.

Founded in 2021 by former OpenAI staffers including chief executive officer Dario Amodei, Anthropic (ANTHRO) was valued at $380B in a $30B funding round co-led by MGX that closed in February. The company has partnerships with Alphabet’s Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA). These established firms have taken stakes in the AI startup, and have given Anthropic specialized chips and other technology in deals worth tens of billions of dollars.

Earlier this year, Anthropic (ANTHRO) was designated by the Pentagon as a threat to the U.S. supply chain under an authority typically reserved for foreign adversaries. However, the company secured a court order on Thursday blocking the restriction on government use of its technology, arguing the move could cost it billions in lost revenue.

Anthropic signed a $200M contract with the United States Department of Defense in July, becoming the first lab to deploy its models in mission workflows on classified networks. Rivals OpenAI (OPENAI), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and xAI (X.AI) also received DoD awards worth up to $200M last year.

data.title: Bitmine Immersion Technologies acquires Pier Two to power MAVAN staking launch
Trump’s signature to appear on U.S. currency, a first for a sitting president
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump’s signature will appear on future U.S. paper currency to mark the country’s 250th anniversary, in what would be a first for a sitting president, according to the Treasury Department.

“There is no more powerful way to recognize the historic achievements of our great country and President Donald J. Trump than U.S. dollar bills bearing his name,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

U.S. currency has traditionally carried the signatures of the Treasury secretary and the treasurer.

The move comes as Trump has sought to shape the nation’s semiquincentennial celebrations this year, including plans to host a UFC fight at the White House.

Trump’s allies have also pushed to attach his name to major institutions while he remains in office, including the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in Washington and an airport in Palm Beach, Florida.

Printing Trump’s signature on U.S. currency “is not only appropriate, but also well deserved,” U.S. Treasurer Brandon Beach said.

Beach added in October that the department was also considering minting a $1 coin featuring Trump’s image.

Dear readers, We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump to sign order to pay TSA workers during shutdown
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said he plans to sign an order to pay Transportation Security Administration ((TSA)) officers, aiming to ease airport disruptions as Congress remains deadlocked over ending a partial government shutdown.

In a social media post Thursday, “I am going to sign an Order instructing the Secretary of Homeland Security, Markwayne Mullin, to immediately pay our TSA Agents in order to address this Emergency Situation, and to quickly stop the Democrat Chaos at the Airports. It is not an easy thing to do, but I am going to do it!”

According to a senior administration official, the payments will be funded using money from Trump’s 2025 tax and spending bill. The White House used a similar approach last year to pay military personnel during a broader shutdown.

The move sidesteps the regular congressional funding process and could face legal challenges.

The action comes amid a weeks-long funding standoff over the Department of Homeland Security, which oversees the TSA, leading to staffing shortages and longer wait times at airport checkpoints.

Immigration and Customs Enforcement ((ICE)) agents have been deployed at airports to help ease delays, and ICE and border patrol personnel have continued to receive pay through the shutdown due to additional funding allocated in the July spending bill. However, other DHS workers, including TSA officers, have gone unpaid for weeks, disrupting travel at major hubs and adding pressure to an economy already dealing with higher energy prices linked to the Iran conflict.

Negotiations in Congress have intensified as lawmakers head into a two-week break. Democrats have held up DHS funding in a bid to secure changes to immigration policies following the killings of two US citizens by agents earlier this year, while Republicans have rejected proposals from Democrats for a standalone bill to fund TSA operations and parts of the DHS that are not related to immigration.

Dear readers, We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance)

This monthly report offers advance import and export data on the goods components of the monthly trade report.

10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

The consensus looks for sentiment to be revised down to 54.3 in the final March report from 55.5 in the preliminary March reading due largely to rising energy prices flowing from the Iran war.

11:00 AM Thomas Barkin Speaks 

Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Thomas Barkin speaks before the Appalachian Highlands Economic Forum.

11:30 AM Mary Daly Speaks 

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly gives introductory remarks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

11:35 AM Anna Paulson Speaks

Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Anna Paulson speaks before the Macroeconomics and Monetary Policy Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: Oxford Industries outlines $1.475B-$1.53B 2026 sales outlook as sourcing shifts drive margin strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oxford Industries (OXM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Chubb stated that fourth quarter net sales and adjusted earnings per share, “helped by late January momentum of our largest brand, Tommy Bahama, landed at the midpoint of our guidance ranges, excluding charges associated with the bankruptcy of Saks Global that were not known when we last updated our outlook.” He pointed to a challenging holiday season with “pressured traffic and conversion trends across much of our portfolio and a highly promotional marketplace,” but highlighted late-quarter improvements driven by actions to strengthen the business, particularly in supply chain diversification and inventory management.
  • Chubb emphasized, “Comparable sales led by mid-single-digit positive comps at Tommy Bahama improved and turned positive for the total company in late January.” He noted that Tommy Bahama comps have remained mid-single-digit positive into the first quarter of fiscal 2026, while Lilly Pulitzer comps have underperformed due to colder weather, and Johnny Was comps remain negative but are in line with expectations.
  • Chubb discussed the completion of the Lyons, Georgia distribution center, describing it as “the most significant infrastructure investment Oxford has made in many years.” He also reported that their sourcing from China declined from about 40% at the start of 2025 to approximately 15% annualized entering 2026.
  • The CEO outlined strategic priorities for 2026, including building on Tommy Bahama’s momentum, unlocking more sustainable profitability at Lilly Pulitzer, executing a brand revitalization at Johnny Was, and accelerating growth for the Emerging Brands Group.
  • CFO & COO K. Grassmyer reported, “We finished the fourth quarter and full fiscal year '25 with top line results within our guidance range and bottom line results within our guidance range, excluding $0.19 per share of charges related to the Saks Global bankruptcy.” Grassmyer stated consolidated net sales for 2025 decreased 3% to $1.48 billion, with declines in full-price brick-and-mortar and e-commerce, but growth in food and beverage locations.

Outlook

  • Management expects 2026 net sales between $1.475 billion and $1.53 billion, “approximately flat to up 4% compared to sales of $1.478 billion in 2025.” The plan anticipates comp sales “approximately flat to positive 3%,” with growth in the Tommy Bahama, Lilly Pulitzer, and Emerging Brands segments, and a decrease at Johnny Was.
  • The company assumes continued tariff pressures, with “IEEPA-related tariff headwinds of $50 million during fiscal '26 or an incremental $20 million or 150 basis points of gross margin impact and $1 per share impact.”
  • Adjusted EPS for 2026 is projected at $2.10 to $2.70 versus adjusted EPS of $2.11 in 2025 (which included $0.19 of Saks Global bankruptcy charges).
  • For Q1 2026, sales are expected in the range of $385 million to $395 million, with adjusted EPS between $1.20 and $1.30 compared to $1.82 in Q1 2025, impacted by higher tariffs and a more promotional environment.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales in fiscal 2025 were $1.48 billion, down 3% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted gross margin contracted 190 basis points to 61.3%, “driven primarily by higher tariffs of $30 million or 200 basis points.”
  • Adjusted SG&A expenses increased 4% to $815 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $107 million or 7.2% margin compared to $193 million or 12.7% margin in the prior year.
  • Adjusted EPS for the year was $2.11, including $0.19 of charges related to the Saks Global bankruptcy.
  • Inventory at year-end decreased 1% on a LIFO basis and increased 2% on a FIFO basis, with higher inventory costs tied to tariffs.
  • Long-term debt increased to $116 million, up from $31 million at the end of the prior year.
  • The company generated $120 million of cash flow from operations, with capital expenditures of $108 million, $55 million in share repurchases, and $42 million in dividends.

Q&A

  • Ashley Owens, KeyBanc: Asked about Tommy Bahama’s mid-single-digit comp momentum and drivers. Chubb responded that consistent results are being driven by “having the right product in the right depth in the stores,” highlighting improvements in core men’s products and new women’s offerings. He noted, “the West has really been kind of on fire … that bodes well for our ability to sustain this momentum.”
  • Owens followed with a question on gross margin impact from DTC and food and beverage channel mix. Grassmyer stated, “with DTC growing and wholesale, we talked about pulling back a little bit. It certainly helps the gross margin. … when the DTC is coming into a comp, it really falls to the bottom line.”
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory: Inquired about wholesale strategy post-Saks, Florida performance, CapEx, and margin outlook. Chubb clarified, “Florida is actually getting stronger. It’s improving. ... the West is on fire.” On wholesale, he cited opportunities with Bloomingdale’s, Macy’s, Dillard’s, and Nordstrom. Grassmyer added that lower CapEx will allow for “a meaningful buy down of our debt level,” and margin leverage is possible if sales reach the high end of guidance.
  • Ethan Saghi, BTIG: Asked about debt reduction targets and Johnny Was brand revitalization. Grassmyer noted a planned $30 million to $40 million debt reduction. Chubb detailed Johnny Was actions including “better storytelling,” product innovation, and adjusting price points, with merchandising tools already driving improved weekly KPIs.
  • Mauricio Serna Vega, UBS: Queried about the acceleration implied in guidance and Lilly Pulitzer’s outlook. Grassmyer tied softer Q1 comps to cold weather, especially in Florida, expecting improvement as conditions normalize. Chubb noted, “Lilly is so dependent on Florida … when the average temperature is colder than normal ... comps tend to be weak.”
  • Tracy Kogan, Citigroup: Asked about inventory planning and the impact of the Lyons facility. Chubb stated inventory optimization tools are being rolled out across brands, with the most impact expected in 2027. Grassmyer said the Lyons DC will “lead to once we have the rhythm to us being able to carry less inventory.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on sustainability of recent improvements, margin levers, channel mix, and brand-specific recovery, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on execution and guidance reconciliation.
  • Management remained confident in prepared remarks, stating, “we are excited about what we’re seeing in Tommy Bahama,” but acknowledged persistent headwinds, especially tariffs and promotional pressure. During Q&A, management’s tone was measured and focused on operational execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts’ tone shifted from cautious and probing about assortment gaps and tariffs to cautiously optimistic regarding recent positive trends. Management’s tone improved from defensive around fourth quarter challenges to a more constructive outlook built on recent gains and operational progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from defensive and focused on downside risk in Q3 to an emphasis on building momentum, particularly at Tommy Bahama, and an expectation of gross margin expansion despite continued tariff headwinds.
  • Strategic focus evolved from mitigating assortment and sourcing issues to leveraging new infrastructure and merchandising analytics for future gains.
  • Analysts’ questions in Q4 centered more on sustainability and execution, whereas Q3 was focused on understanding the causes and expected duration of recent weakness.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA and EPS declined year-over-year, but guidance for 2026 sales and profitability was framed around stabilization and incremental improvement.
  • Management’s confidence increased in describing actions taken and early signs of recovery in core brands.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued uncertainty in the consumer environment, ongoing tariff pressures, and potential macro risks such as the conflict involving Iran and oil price impacts on consumer spending and costs.
  • Grassmyer stated, “we are not incorporating any benefit from the recent Supreme Court decision or any related subsequent actions on other tariff matters,” and guidance includes $50 million in tariff headwinds for 2026.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the pace of recovery at Johnny Was, the sustainability of Tommy Bahama’s comp momentum, margin headwinds from tariffs and promotions, and the impact of colder weather on Lilly Pulitzer.

Final Takeaway

Oxford Industries’ management reported that actions to improve sourcing, invest in infrastructure, and optimize merchandising are beginning to drive positive momentum, particularly at Tommy Bahama. Despite ongoing tariff and macroeconomic pressures, the company projects flat to modest sales growth and aims for a return to earnings growth in 2026, with further improvements expected as merchandising analytics and the new distribution center reach full scale. Management remains focused on operational discipline, cash generation, and long-term brand strength to navigate an uncertain environment and position the company for future gains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Xos outlines 2026 guidance of $40M–$50M revenue and 350–500 deliveries while expanding hub and powertrain platforms
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Xos, Inc. (XOS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Dakota Semler, CEO, stated that "2025 was the year Xos proved something that many doubted was possible, that a young electric vehicle company operating with discipline under real constraints and without the luxury of unlimited capital could deliver a full year of positive free cash flow, grow its customer base, diversify its product portfolio and emerge stronger on the other side." He detailed that Xos generated $46 million in revenue on 328 units delivered and marked its second consecutive full year of positive GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins.
  • Semler highlighted that "we generated $5.4 million of positive free cash flow for the year compared to negative $49.1 million in 2024. That is a $54 million swing. And in Q4, we delivered our third consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow." He emphasized demand from large fleets like UPS and FedEx ISPs and the ongoing fulfillment of a 200-plus unit program.
  • He announced a strategic restructuring with Aljomaih Automotive Company, extending the convertible note's maturity from August 2025 to quarterly installments through February 2028, describing this as "a strategic alignment that allows us to operate from a position of focus rather than constraint. Aljomaih is now our largest shareholder."
  • Xos expanded its product portfolio, with the powertrain business delivering 15 powertrain systems to Blue Bird Corporation in Q4 and receiving nearly 100 additional orders since Q2. The Xos Hub product line is being upgraded for 2026, with new configurations ranging from 210 to 630-kilowatt hours, positioning Xos as "an energy company, not just an electric vehicle company."
  • Giordano Sordoni, COO, described 2025 as "a year defined by focused execution, operational discipline and continued progress towards scalable production," discussing manufacturing expansion, supply chain resilience, and a 28% reduction in operating expenses.
  • Acting CFO Liana Pogosyan stated, "This was a year of execution and important milestones across the business from achieving positive free cash flow for the full year and improving liquidity to driving substantial reductions in operating losses and expenses." She specified that "for the fourth quarter 2025, revenue was $5.2 million on 34 units," with operating expenses at $7.1 million and full year EBITDA loss improving to $21 million from $42.2 million in 2024.

Outlook

  • Pogosyan provided 2026 guidance: "We anticipate revenue to fall within the range of $40 million to $50 million, unit deliveries to be within the range of 350 and 500 and non-GAAP operating loss to be in the range of $11.9 million to $13.3 million."
  • Management indicated that the ongoing shift in product mix toward powertrains and hubs is expected to continue, with double-digit or higher growth rates for these segments.

Financial Results

  • Xos reported $5.2 million in Q4 revenue on 34 units, compared to $16.5 million on 130 units last quarter and $11.5 million on 51 units a year ago. Full year revenue was $46 million on 328 units, down from $56 million on 297 units last year.
  • GAAP gross margin for the year was $2.7 million or 5.9%. Fourth quarter GAAP gross margin was a loss of $2.6 million, with non-GAAP gross margin at a profit of $0.3 million or 5.2%.
  • Full year operating expenses were $35.8 million, down 28% from last year. Operating loss narrowed by 28% to $33.1 million. Year-end cash was $14 million, up from $11 million, and inventory declined to $25 million from $36.6 million.
  • Xos achieved positive free cash flow of $5.4 million for the year, with Q4 free cash flow at $2.4 million.

Q&A

  • Andrew Scutt, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about the new hub product opportunities outside traditional EV charging. Semler described diverse customer use cases, including as "a direct replacement for large DC charging infrastructure sites," for remote utility power, and for disaster preparedness. The hub's new configurations target light-duty, flagship, and heavy-duty applications, with additional product upgrades planned for Q2 and Q3.
  • Scutt asked about expanding the powertrain product line. Semler explained that Xos is leveraging engineering synergies to drive cost competitiveness and reliability, stating, "We're developing several configurations... addressing the traditional Type C school buses... but we are also developing a rear engine configuration... and we're really focused on commonizing our platforms."
  • Edward Jackson, Northland Capital Markets: Inquired about unit mix and future shifts. Pogosyan said, "the majority of the units were predominantly StepVan's and hubs and powertrains made up the remainder of the mix for the full year. For the fourth quarter, powertrain and hubs drove the significant volumes." Semler added, "the rate of growth that we're seeing in both the hub business and the powertrain business is high double digits and could easily exceed triple digits this year."
  • Jackson asked about margin improvement due to product mix. Semler noted hubs are the highest gross margin product and said, "we anticipate that our 2026 pricing... will allow us to achieve those target margin ranges without having to go back to the customer and have them make concessions or share in any additional tariff exposure."
  • Jackson also queried about the UPS program and Blue Bird powertrain shipments. Semler responded, "the vast majority of them have shipped... only a few units that will hit this quarter," and with Blue Bird, "we do anticipate that business will grow... probably double digits, if not triple digits in terms of percentage this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on product mix, margin improvement, and working capital outlook, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, acknowledging operational progress but probing on sustainability and segment growth. Jackson described the working capital improvement as "unbelievable, amazing" but probed whether further gains are possible.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and responses. Semler used phrases such as "the foundation is built, now it is the time to scale" and "we are building a business that is increasingly self-sustaining."
  • Compared to Q3, analysts' tone remains measured but constructive; management's tone remains confident, with more emphasis on scalability and segment expansion versus prior quarters' focus on risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted from record Q3 deliveries and revenue momentum to a seasonally lighter Q4, with operating performance and cost discipline sustaining. Q3 revenue was $16.5 million on 130 units, while Q4 was $5.2 million on 34 units.
  • Guidance language shifted from reaffirming 2025 targets in Q3 to providing a specific 2026 range, reflecting greater confidence in segment growth (hubs and powertrains) and ongoing cost control.
  • Management's tone moved from emphasizing resilience and discipline in Q3 to scaling and expanding product reach in Q4. Analysts' focus evolved from margin mix and working capital to deeper questions on segment-specific growth and product innovation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited risks from supply chain disruptions, tariff changes, and macroeconomic volatility.
  • Semler highlighted the strategic restructuring of debt to extend runway, and discussed ongoing cost discipline, supplier diversification, and inventory management as mitigation strategies.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the sustainability of working capital improvements and the continued margin pressure from product mix and tariff exposure.

Final Takeaway

Xos management emphasized that 2025 marked a turning point, with the company achieving positive free cash flow, broadening its product suite, and strengthening its balance sheet through cost discipline and strategic partnerships. Looking to 2026, Xos targets $40 million to $50 million in revenue and 350 to 500 deliveries, with major growth expected in the hub and powertrain segments. The company is preparing to scale its operations, extend its energy solutions, and deepen relationships with fleet and OEM partners while maintaining a focus on liquidity and margin expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Insight Molecular Diagnostics outlines $2B annual market opportunity and signals rapid commercialization push for GraftAssureDx
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insight Molecular Diagnostics Inc. (IMDX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joshua Riggs announced the submission of GraftAssureDx to the FDA, emphasizing, “we are, to our knowledge, the first dd-cfDNA assay to be submitted to the FDA for authorization.” Riggs detailed a shift in company focus toward product launch and commercialization, stating the company is accelerating adoption plans post-authorization. He highlighted strong engagement with transplant centers: “Since we announced the registry in the fall, we've had 28 centers start the process to become a trial site. That's over halfway to our goal of 50 in a very short period of time.”
  • Riggs noted strategic investments in clinical, technical sales, and medical affairs headcount to drive engagement and awareness. Early head-to-head data was described as pivotal, with a major transplant center presenting “preliminary data showing that they could get equivalent results to one of the national reference labs using our GraftAssure technology.”
  • CEO Riggs also outlined 2026 goals, including launching GraftAssure research use only, marketing GraftAssure core, and driving samples to their Tennessee lab. Additional initiatives include submitting GraftAssure for approval in the U.K. and EU, and pivoting R&D to heart transplant testing.
  • CFO Andrea James stated, “we are preparing to do two focus things. First, we intend to commercialize and begin scaling that kidney transplant assay. And so accordingly, we're making controlled investments in sales and marketing to do that. And second, we intend to maintain our product development momentum by expanding GraftAssure into heart transplant testing.” She reported completion of a $26 million offering and ongoing disciplined investment, adding, “maintenance spending is about $5.5 million per quarter, and our growth spending is about $2 million per quarter.” James highlighted a $2 billion annual total addressable market and discussed updated Medicare reimbursement for GraftAssure at $27.53.

Outlook

  • The company’s 2026 objectives focus on expanding market access, launching research use only and core versions of GraftAssure, increasing sample volume at their Tennessee lab, and advancing regulatory submissions in the U.K. and EU.
  • Management reiterated plans to expand into heart transplant testing and indicated ongoing investments to support these activities. James noted, “Those expenses should come back down again in Q1 to a number that's between the Q3 and Q4 levels.”
  • The company expects low revenue from lab services in 2026 and almost no revenue in the first quarter.

Financial Results

  • CFO James reported a recent $26 million capital raise and described continued careful management of maintenance and growth spending. She stated, “We believe that our maintenance spending is covered at about $35 million a year in revenue, as if we assume a software-like gross margin, that would ramp over time to get to that level on GraftAssure.”
  • James disclosed an intangible asset write-down in Q4 related to oncology assets, stating, “We back out of adjusted EBITDA… is tied to writing down the intangible value of the remainder of our oncology assets.” She confirmed the introduction of non-GAAP EPS and adjusted EBITDA tables this year.
  • The company highlighted a growing sales pipeline, reporting engagement with 37 U.S. transplant centers and a potential average of $1 million in annual revenue per center.

Q&A

  • Thomas Flaten, Lake Street Capital: Asked about the revenue impact of registry participation, given 37 engaged centers and 28 in the registry. Riggs responded, “we're targeting is about 100 patients per center… we expect to test each of those patients about 10x over the course of the study,” adding that “the goal with the registry is that it's self-funding, but not that it itself is a profit center.”
  • Flaten also questioned the Bio-Rad commercialization agreement timeline. Riggs clarified, “explicit language in the agreement is a 90-day right of first negotiation post authorization.”
  • Michael Matson, Needham: Inquired about heart vs. liver testing strategy. Riggs explained, “demand in heart is much stronger for a test… If you start rejecting a heart, there's a very high risk of death and so the testing demand there is just higher.” Chief Science Officer Ekkehard Schutz outlined the heart study and FDA pathway, stating, “we are right now in touch with, I think, 4 or 5 hard transplant centers in the U.S. for a smaller study, perhaps 150 people patients so that we can provide for CMS approval.”
  • Matson asked about the impact of shifting to patient management. Riggs emphasized, “the data that we've generated in our randomized interventional study showing that we can pick up rejection, 10 to 11 months ahead of standard of care protocol is important here.”
  • Matson followed up on cash burn and OpEx trends. James specified, “we told you last year, we were averaging about $6 million a quarter. We're now up to $7.5 million, but it's over $8 million in the first half, and then we're going to come back down to hopefully closer to $6 million in the back half.”
  • Benjamin Mee, Stephens: Asked about billing and coding post-authorization. Riggs detailed, “we are submitting for a Z code specifically for the regulated version of our test… when somebody inside of MolDx like Vanderbilt… would use that Z code.”
  • Mee also asked for feedback from validation studies. Riggs recommended a recent large-center presentation and noted, “once they show that they can get equivalent results, then they get to reap all the benefits of having it in-house.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a constructive tone, focusing on commercial strategy, registry participation, and validation data, with questions centering on adoption hurdles and revenue potential.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks, with Riggs stating, “We believe that… our assay becoming the de facto standard dd-cfDNA testing provider to the market.” During Q&A, management maintained clarity and transparency, directly addressing commercialization, partnership, and operational plans.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence increased as the FDA submission milestone was achieved. Analyst tone remained focused on execution and conversion prospects but was slightly more bullish given the company’s progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a major milestone with the FDA submission of GraftAssureDx, while the previous quarter was focused on nearing submission and clinical trial progress.
  • Strategic focus shifted from product development and trial execution to commercialization, registry engagement, and operational scaling.
  • Analysts’ attention moved from regulatory risks and trial enrollment to revenue generation from registries, partnership negotiations, and commercialization strategies.
  • Management’s tone evolved from cautious optimism regarding regulatory timelines to assertive confidence about market positioning and execution.
  • Key metric changes included a $26 million capital raise this quarter, expanded clinical engagement, and a $2 billion updated TAM projection.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the challenge of clinician adoption, with Riggs noting, “Convincing doctors to change what they're doing isn't always the easiest thing.”
  • James reported increased Q4 R&D expenses due to FDA submission and clinical trial costs, with expectations that these expenses will decline in Q1.
  • The company anticipates low lab services revenue in 2026 and almost none in Q1, highlighting the need for successful commercialization.
  • Reimbursement in the U.K. and EU remains uncertain, though “demand is clearly there for a kitted product.”
  • The transition to heart transplant testing introduces new regulatory and CMS approval steps, but management detailed a streamlined FDA 510(k) pathway.

Final Takeaway

Insight Molecular Diagnostics management reported the successful FDA submission of GraftAssureDx and outlined a sharp pivot toward commercial execution, targeting robust registry engagement, expansion into heart transplant testing, and operational scaling to capture a $2 billion annual market opportunity. With newly raised capital, growing validation data, and a focus on converting clinical engagement into in-house adoption, the company emphasized disciplined growth spending and a clear path to self-sustaining revenue, positioning itself for a significant transformation as commercialization approaches.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil edges lower as Trump extends pause on attacking Iran's energy sites
data.description:
U.S. crude oil futures turned lower after President Trump said late Thursday he was extending a pause in attacking Iran's energy facilities by another 10 days, to April 6.

"As per Iranian Government request, please let this statement serve to represent that I am pausing the period of Energy Plant destruction," Trump said in a Truth Social post. "Talks are ongoing and, despite erroneous statements to the contrary by the Fake News Media, and others, they are going very well."

Crude oil jumped in regular trading Thursday as the market maintained doubts about the success of U.S. efforts to negotiate an end to the conflict with Iran.

The president's extension of his pause followed renewed threats to strike Iran if the country failed to "get serious" about talks "before it is too late," while reports indicate the U.S. is moving more troops to the region and Israel said it had killed the Iran Revolutionary Guard's naval commander.

"A chasm still exists between Trump's comments that major progress is being seen in negotiations and that the war has been won, and Iran's indicated refusal to accept U.S. demands," Ritterbusch & Associates said in a note.

Although some safe passage has been offered to a few cargoes through the Strait of Hormuz and the Saudis have stepped up exports through the Red Sea port of Yanbu, "this incremental supply remains modest compared to the disrupted cargo flow through the strait."

Largely crowded out of news reporting is the impact of Ukrainian drone attacks on Russian oil shipping; Reuters calculations based on market data show at least 40% of Russia's oil export capacity has been halted.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery gained 4.6% in regular trading Thursday to $94.48/bbl but has turned ~1% lower after-hours, while front-month Brent crude for May delivery (CO1:COM) climbed 5.6% to $108.01/bbl, its third-highest settlement value this year.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) settled higher, as the Energy Information Administration reported a larger than average withdrawal from storage for last week, in what may have been the last draw of the winter season; the front-month Nymex April contract closed up 1.6% to $2.999/MMBtu.
